content:
Plotly traces its roots to **Montreal-based Plotly Inc.** (founded **2012**), which set out to bring **web-native, interactive charts** to scientific and business analytics. The open-source **plotly.py** package wrapped the JavaScript Plotly.js renderer so Python users could build figures in notebooks and export HTML without writing front-end code.

**Plotly Express** (2019) added a **Seaborn-like, DataFrame-first API**—`px.scatter`, `px.line`, and faceting—lowering the barrier for interactive EDA. **Graph Objects** remained the low-level interface for custom traces and layouts, mirroring the Express-vs-OO split familiar from Matplotlib and Seaborn.

**Dash** (2015 onward) extended Plotly into **reactive web dashboards**: Flask-served apps with React components (`dcc.Graph`, `dcc.Dropdown`) and Python callbacks—adopted by ML teams for experiment browsers and monitoring views.

In the Python ML ecosystem, Plotly filled the gap left by **static Matplotlib/Seaborn** figures: **hover tooltips**, **zoom**, and **self-contained HTML** became standard for embedding reviews, training-curve inspection, and stakeholder demos. Interpretation practices for manifold coordinates stayed in dedicated embedding topics; Plotly supplied the **interaction layer**.

Today Plotly sits alongside—not instead of—static libraries: papers and slides often still use Matplotlib `savefig`, while notebooks and internal tools use Plotly for **explorable** views and lightweight Dash apps.

timeline.label: 2012 — Plotly founded
timeline.description: Plotly Inc. launches with a JavaScript charting library and cloud platform; plotly.py brings the same interactivity to Python notebooks.

timeline.label: 2015 — Dash introduced
timeline.description: Dash framework combines Plotly figures with reactive web components for filter-driven analytics and monitoring dashboards.

timeline.label: 2019 — Plotly Express
timeline.description: High-level px API ships, mapping DataFrame columns to interactive scatter, line, histogram, and facet plots in one call.

timeline.label: 2020s — ML notebook adoption
timeline.description: Interactive training curves, embedding previews, and write_html sharing become common in ML coursework and experiment review workflows.

timeline.label: Present — complementary stack
timeline.description: Matplotlib/Seaborn for static EDA and publication; Plotly for hover-rich exploration and HTML/Dash delivery in gen-ai visualization curricula.
